source.title: In vivo–directed evolution of adeno-associated virus in the primate retina

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Efficient AAV-mediated gene delivery to the outer retina remains a significant obstacle for retinal gene therapies. Existing AAV serotypes have limited transduction efficiency and tropism for photoreceptors and RPE after intravitreal injection, especially in large-animal models with anatomical barriers like the inner limiting membrane. The goal is to evolve AAV capsids capable of delivering genes to the outer retina in primates via intravitreal injection, enabling less invasive and more widespread retinal gene therapy.

section_gists.heading: In vivo-directed evolution of adeno-associated virus in the primate retina
section_gists.gist: The paper applies directed evolution guided by deep sequencing and secondary GFP-barcoded library screening to create AAV capsids that deliver genes to the outer retina in primates after intravitreal injection. A replication-incompetent library was used to mitigate risk. Six rounds of in vivo selection resulted in vectors with redirected tropism and increased gene delivery efficiency to retinal cells.
process_signals: Directed evolution approach was chosen to overcome inefficient gene delivery to outer retina.
Replication-incompetent library was designed to mitigate risk of AAV propagation in primates.
Deep sequencing was used to guide selection and analyze population dynamics.
Secondary GFP-barcoded library screening was performed to identify top-performing variants.
Resulting vectors showed redirected tropism to outer retina and increased efficiency.
methods: Directed evolution with replication-incompetent AAV library
Recovery of genomes from outer retina
metrics: Gene delivery efficiency to retinal cells

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Inherited retinal degenerations are caused by mutations in genes expressed in photoreceptors or RPE. Gene therapy via intravitreal injection is less invasive but inefficient for outer retinal delivery due to anatomical barriers in large animals. Directed evolution has created efficient vectors in mice, but these are less effective in large-animal models. The study aims to use directed evolution in primates to create AAVs for outer retinal gene delivery after intravitreal injection.
process_signals: Problem: inefficient gene delivery to outer retina via intravitreal injection.
Constraint: anatomical barriers in large-animal retinas (e.g., thicker ILM, vitreous consistency) limit transduction.
Hypothesis: directed evolution in primates can yield AAVs with enhanced outer retinal tropism.
Previous work: directed evolution in mice produced vectors, but they underperform in large animals.
Goal: create AAVs capable of delivering genes to outer retina in primates.
methods: Use of cynomolgus macaques as model
metrics: Gene delivery efficiency to outer retina
failures_or_limits: Vectors selected in mice are not efficient in large-animal models.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Directed evolution was performed in primates using replication-incompetent libraries (AAV2-7-mer, Ancestral-7-mer, LoopSwap) with a rep in trans strategy to prevent replication. Libraries were injected intravitreally, and after each round, cap genes were recovered from ONL and RPE, then repackaged for up to 5 rounds, with error-prone PCR after round 3. Deep sequencing showed library diversity decreased from ~10^6-10^7 to ~10^4-10^5 variants over 6 rounds. Variants with greatest fold increase, not highest final frequency, were prioritized. A secondary GFP-barcoded library of 12 variants plus controls was injected, and ONL/RPE were sequenced to rank variants. Top variants included LoopSwap AAV2 583 LQRGVRIPSVLEVNGQ and AAV2-LALIQDSMRA (NHP#9). NHP#9 was validated with SNCG-tdTomato (RGCs) and pR1.7-GFP (cones) in a primate, showing expression in both cell types.
process_signals: Replication-incompetent libraries were used to mitigate risk in primates.
Libraries were injected, harvested, and repackaged for 5 rounds; error-prone PCR after round 3 introduced diversity.
Deep sequencing revealed convergence and identified variants with significant fold increase.
Overrepresentation in initial library masked promising variants; fold increase was better metric.
Secondary GFP-barcoded screen ranked variants for ONL and RPE transduction.
Top variant NHP#9 was validated for RGC and cone transduction.
methods: Recovery of cap genes from ONL and RPE via laser capture microdissection
Repackaging and iterative selection (5 rounds + error-prone PCR)
Deep sequencing (Illumina) to track variant frequencies
Validation with SNCG-tdTomato and pR1.7-GFP constructs
metrics: Fold increase in variant representation over rounds
Percentage of total in recovered library/percentage in AAV library (competition index)
GFP expression in photoreceptors and RPE
failures_or_limits: Some variants had low titers (e.g., top LoopSwap variant ~5e11 vg/mL).
Overrepresentation in initial library confounded selection; required fold-increase metric.
Only 12 variants were tested in secondary screen; many potentially good variants not tested.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Directed evolution guided by deep sequencing identified AAV variants with enhanced outer retinal transduction in primates. Deep sequencing revealed that fold increase, not final frequency, is the optimal metric for identifying top variants, as initial overrepresentation can mask promising variants. The study demonstrates the utility of replication-incompetent libraries for safe in vivo evolution in primates. Limitations include the small number of variants validated and the need for further studies in disease models and humans.
process_signals: Deep sequencing was crucial for understanding selection dynamics and avoiding bias from initial overrepresentation.
Fold increase metric was key to identifying truly enriched variants.
Replication-incompetent libraries enabled safe in vivo selection in primates.
The approach can be applied to other tissues and species.
methods: Deep sequencing analysis of selection rounds
failures_or_limits: Only a subset of variants was validated; many potentially effective variants remain untested.
Long-term expression and safety not assessed.
Primate model may not fully predict human response.
Immune suppression used in validation may affect translatability.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Detailed methods for library construction (pRepSafeStop with stop codons in rep, pRepIntronHelper with intron in rep supplied in trans), adenovirus rescue assay to confirm reduced replication, AAV selection in primate outer retina (intravitreal injection, tissue harvesting, laser capture microdissection of ONL and RPE, DNA extraction, PCR recovery of cap genes, recloning, repackaging), and AAV packaging. Also includes Table 1 ranking variants from GFP-barcoded screen.
process_signals: pRepSafeStop backbone introduced stop codons in rep to prevent Rep protein expression.
pRepIntronHelper supplied rep with an intron to prevent packaging of recombined genomes.
Adenovirus rescue assay confirmed >10-fold reduction in replication.
Selection involved 3-week intervals, euthanasia, retinal dissection, and layer-specific DNA extraction.
Unique restriction sites (AscI, SpeI) maintained library separation during rounds.
Table 1 shows quantitative ranking of variants in ONL and RPE.
methods: Intron insertion from nebulin gene into rep helper
Adenovirus rescue assay to quantify replication
Retinal sectioning and laser capture microdissection
PCR recovery of cap genes with library-specific primers
Triple transient transfection for AAV production
metrics: Variant ranking by competition index (percentage of total in recovered/percentage in library)
failures_or_limits: Some variants had low production titers.
The replication-incompetent system still had residual recombination risk.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study aimed to overcome the inefficiency of intravitreal AAV delivery to the outer retina in primates by using directed evolution.
The hypothesis was that iterative in vivo selection in the primate retina would yield capsid variants with enhanced tropism for photoreceptors and RPE.
Deep sequencing was hypothesized to reveal selection dynamics and identify variants with the greatest fitness (fold increase) rather than those simply overrepresented initially.
A replication-incompetent library was hypothesized to enable safe in vivo evolution without risk of AAV propagation.
Secondary screening with a barcoded library was hypothesized to directly compare the performance of top candidates.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the specific sequences and designs of the initial libraries (AAV2-7-mer, Ancestral-7-mer, LoopSwap)?
What were the exact selection pressures and how did they change over rounds?
Were there any rounds where no enrichment was observed, or where diversity crashed?
How were the 12 variants for secondary screening chosen from the thousands of enriched variants?
What was the performance of other top variants besides NHP#9 in validation?
Were there any off-target effects or immune responses observed?
How does the transduction efficiency compare to subretinal injection?
What is the durability of expression and long-term safety?

events.summary: Efficient AAV-mediated gene delivery to the outer retina remains a significant obstacle for retinal gene therapies.
A replication-incompetent library was designed to mitigate risk of AAV propagation in primates by providing rep in trans.
Replication-incompetent libraries were constructed, packaged, and included in the primate screen.
Libraries were injected, harvested, and repackaged for up to 5 sequential rounds of selection, with error-prone PCR after round 3.
A subset of variants increased significantly in representation during selection, despite initial overrepresentation of some library members.
A secondary GFP-barcoded library of 12 variants plus controls was injected in primates to rank variants for ONL and RPE transduction.
Top variant NHP#9 showed increased cone transduction and decreased RGC transduction compared to 7m8, indicating a tropism shift toward outer retina.
numbers_or_metrics: 11.71-fold increase in GFP expression in foveal cones

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, results including library design, selection rounds, deep sequencing analysis, secondary screening, and initial validation of top variants.

events.summary: Workflow of directed evolution of AAV in the primate retina is described, including library injection, tissue harvesting, PCR recovery, recloning, repackaging, and deep sequencing.
Secondary screening with GFP-barcoded library to rank variants based on fold increase in barcode representation.
Deep sequencing revealed that fold increase, not final frequency, is the optimal metric for identifying top-performing variants.
Overrepresentation in the original library masked promising variants; fold increase metric was necessary.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains Figure 2 workflow description, secondary screening design, key analysis insight about fold increase metric, and validation expression data.

events.summary: Deep sequencing revealed convergence of variants over rounds of selection.
A small proportion of variants were overrepresented in the plasmid library.
Scatterplots show variant behavior over selection rounds, highlighting variants with greatest fold increase.
GFP-barcoded library screening enabled selection of variants with best transduction efficiency for targeted cells.
Variant NHP#9 produced a 2-fold increase in foveal cones infected and ~12-fold increase in GFP expression relative to 7m8.
numbers_or_metrics: 2-fold increase in number of foveal cones infected

events.summary: Further work is required to determine optimal immune suppression regimen.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains deep sequencing analysis, scatterplot interpretation, GFP-barcoded screening results, validation metrics for NHP#9 and NHP#26, and methods for replication-incompetent backbone construction.

events.summary: AAV selection in primate outer retina: three weeks after intravitreal injection, the primate was euthanized, eyes fixed, retinal regions dissected, RPE separated, and sections prepared for DNA extraction.
methods_or_tools: QIAGEN DNeasy blood and tissue kit

events.summary: Capsid sequences were recovered by PCR from harvested cells using library-specific primers to maintain separation of groups of libraries, then recloned into pRepSafeStop backbone.
DNase-resistant viral genomic titers were measured by qRT-PCR using a Bio-Rad iCycler.
Deep sequencing of AAV libraries was performed on an Illumina HiSeq 2500, and custom Python code with Pandas was used to analyze variant dynamics.
Validation of NHP#9 in primate retina: coinjection of SNCG-tdTomato and pR1.7-eGFP packaged in 7m8 and NHP#9 showed reduced ganglion cell expression and increased cone expression for NHP#9.
Validation of NHP#26 in primate retina: injection of NHP#26-scCAG-GFP showed GFP expression in fovea, Müller cells, photoreceptors, and inner nuclear layer cells.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for AAV selection, packaging, deep sequencing, and validation experiments in primates, directly supporting the directed evolution process.

events.summary: GFP-barcoded library was constructed by cloning unique 25-bp DNA barcodes behind a self-complementary AAV ITR construct with CAG promoter driving eGFP.
Individual variants were packaged separately with different barcodes, titer matched, and mixed in equal ratios before injection into primates.
Deep sequencing of GFP-barcoded libraries was performed by PCR amplifying barcodes from retinal tissue DNA, sequencing on MiSeq, and analyzing barcode abundance with custom Python code.
Best performing variants were selected based on fold increase in percent of total library relative to injected library, using competition index.
numbers_or_metrics: percentage of total in recovered sample/percentage of total in injected library

events.summary: Immune suppression was used in primate validation to prevent immune response to GFP or viral vectors, with cyclosporine dosing adjusted based on blood trough levels.
Primates were screened for neutralizing antibodies prior to inclusion, with titers less than 1:25.
Analysis of GFP-barcoded library was performed on only one primate (n=1).

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for GFP-barcoded library construction, deep sequencing analysis, gene expression analysis, and animal study procedures, including immune suppression and antibody screening. It also includes a limitation regarding sample size.

evidence_bank.quote: Efficient adeno-associated virus-mediated (AAV-mediated) gene delivery remains a significant obstacle to effective retinal gene therapies.

To mitigate the possibility of replication of the AAV libraries in the primate retina and subsequent spread, a rep in trans strategy was developed in which the library rep sequence is mutated (pRepSafeStop) while leaving intact regulatory elements, including the P40 promoter, which is necessary for cap protein expression.
Stop codons in the rep sequence prevented the expression of the 4 Rep proteins, and the full rep sequence was supplied on pRepIntronHelper.
an intron was inserted in the rep supplied in trans to prevent packaging of recombined genomes
Replication-incompetent libraries were constructed, packaged, and included in the primate screen, including AAV2-7-mer, Ancestral-7-mer (14), and LoopSwap (15) libraries.
Libraries were injected, harvested, and repackaged for up to 5 sequential rounds of selection, with 1 round of error-prone PCR performed after round 3

analysis of results from deep sequencing over the rounds of selection revealed a subset of variants that increased significantly in their representation during rounds of selection for each of the input libraries
Twelve variants from the three successfully amplified libraries were chosen for a secondary round of selection with GFP-barcoded libraries, along with parental serotypes as controls.
NHP#9 infected 2202 cones, compared with 1019 cones infected by 7m8
Libraries were pooled and injected intravitreally in primates.
After AAV infection had occurred, retinal tissue and RPE cells were collected, and cap gene variants were PCR amplified, recloned, and repackaged for the subsequent round of injection.
Five rounds of selection were performed, and error-prone PCR was performed after the third round to introduce additional diversity into the library.
After the selections, each pool was subjected to deep sequencing to analyze the dynamics of each individual variant and overall convergence of the library.

Based on their increase in representation relative to the original library, individual variant capsids were chosen and used to package a scCAG-eGFP genome also containing a unique DNA barcode sequence.
These barcoded vectors were then pooled in equal amounts and injected intravitreally.
Retinal cells (photoreceptors or RPE cells) were harvested, GFP barcodes were PCR amplified from the collected tissue, and deep sequencing was used to quantify the relative abundance of barcodes.
The top-performing variants were evident because those with the greatest fold increase of barcodes recovered from collected tissue relative to the injected library.

Deep sequencing to gain insights into selection dynamics revealed that the variants with the greatest fold increase during selection, rather than the most frequent final variant, is the optimal metric for identifying top-performing variants.

Overrepresentation of variants in the original library significantly influenced the number of clones in the final round of selection, with variants that were highly represented in the original library more likely to constitute a majority of clones in the final round and mask more promising variants that were less abundant at the start but that had a greater fold increase over rounds of selection.
Deep sequencing of libraries revealed convergence of variants over rounds of selection.
In each of the libraries evaluated, a small proportion of variants were overrepresented in the plasmid library.
Variants that had the greatest fold increase in representation in the final round of selection are shown in magenta.

the use of GFP-barcoded libraries enabled the selection of AAV variants, from a pool of top-performing candidates identified from deep sequencing, with the best transduction efficiency for the targeted cells.
AAV variant NHP#9 produced a 2-fold increase in the number of foveal cones infected and an approximately 12-fold increase of GFP expression in foveal cones relative to 7m8.
Further work is required to determine an optimal regimen and the period during which immune suppression may be required, or if immune suppression may be tapered off after an initial critical period.
Three weeks after intravitreal injection, the primate was euthanized, and both eyes, as whole globes, were briefly submerged in 4% paraformaldehyde.
Retinal sections were then immersed in 30% sucrose, embedded in OCT media, and flash frozen.
DNA was extracted from samples using a QIAGEN DNeasy blood and tissue kit, according to manufacturer's instructions.
PCR amplicons were then digested and recloned into the AAV pRepSafeStop backbone.
DNase-resistant viral genomic titers were measured by qRT-PCR using a Bio-Rad iCycler.
PCR amplicons were purified and sequenced with a 100-cycle single-read run on an Illumina HiSeq 2500.
Custom Python code was written for analysis.
Pandas was used to analyze dynamics of directed evolution and create plots.

Counting of labeled cells, performed using Imaris software, revealed a substantial decrease in the numbers of transduced ganglion cells and an increase in the number of cones targeted with NHP#9, compared with 7m8.
Confocal imaging of native GFP expression in the flat-mounted fovea.

Anti-GFP labeling in a cryostat section revealed GFP expression in photoreceptors, as evidenced by their outer segments, Müller cells, and retina-spanning processes as well as cells in the inner nuclear layer with horizontal processes that are likely interneurons.
Unique 25-bp DNA barcodes were cloned behind a self-complementary AAV ITR construct containing a CAG promoter driving eGFP (CAG-GFP-Barcode-pA).
Individual variants were packaged separately with constructs containing different barcodes. Variants were then titer matched and mixed in equal ratios before injection into primates.
Barcodes were PCR amplified directly from DNA, which was harvested from primate retinal tissue.
PCR amplicons were purified and sequenced with a 100-cycle single-read run on a MiSeq.
Analysis of barcode abundance was performed using custom code written in Python, followed by creation of plots in Pandas.

Best performing variants were selected based on the fold increase in the percent of total library, relative to the injected library (percentage of total in recovered sample/percentage of total in injected library).

To prevent any immune response to GFP or viral vectors, which have been previously reported (7), the monkey used for fluorophore expression received daily subcutaneous injections of cyclosporine at a dose of 6 mg/kg for immune suppression, beginning 1 week before AAV injection, and the dosage regimen was adjusted based on blood trough levels to within a 150–200 ng/ml target range.
All primates were screened for neutralizing antibodies prior to inclusion in the study and had titers less than 1:25.
Analysis was performed on n = 1 primate.
From the last round of selection, sequencing was performed on samples from central (R5C, Supplemental Figure 2) and peripheral (R5P) samples separately.
HEK293T cells were infected with AAV (AAV2, an EP9 variant and a 7-mer Ancestral variant) at a MOI of $10^{5}$ and then incubated at $37^{\circ}$ C and 5% CO $_{2}$ for 48 hours.

AAV libraries were constructed prior to this study and have been previously described (7, 13, 27, 28).

The top-ranking variant from the GFP-barcoded screen, LoopSwap variant \~583-LQRGVRIPSV-LEVNGQ (designated NHP#26), was also tested for validation despite the limitation that reduced production of this vector enabled only a low dose.
The top-ranking vector, LoopSwap variant AAV2 583\~LQRGVRIPSVLEVNGQ, outperformed other variants in the GFP-barcoded screen, though it yielded lower viral titers ( $\sim5 \times 10^{11}$ vg/mL).
LCB, MV, DVS, and JGF are inventors on a patent application for AAV capsid variants (AAV virions with variant capsid and methods of use thereof) (US8663624B2, EP2699270B1 [7m8 patent]).

transfection of HEK293T cells (from ATCC) with the addition of the pRepIntronHelper plasmid in 5 times greater concentration than the library plasmid, purified via iodixanol density centrifugation, and buffer exchanged into PBS by Amicon filtration.
The variant NHP#26 transduced the outer retina at a titer of $5 \times 10^{10}$ vg, 2 log units lower than the dosage used in a previous study to inject a primate with 7m8 (7).
Barcoded-library screening represents a sensitive method for evaluating many variants in parallel, in the same animal, allowing for direct, head-to-head comparison, thus reducing animal numbers (23).
The black dot in the AAV2-7-mer plot indicates variant NHP#9, validated in Figure 5.
Black dots in the LoopSwap plots indicate variant NHP#26, validated in Figure 6.
Although the number of particles injected was 30-fold lower than for the other tested vectors, efficient expression of GFP was observed in the fovea and in regions across the retina (Figure 6).
qRT-PCR, performed using the $\Delta\Delta$ CT method, revealed an 11.71-fold (10.

NHP#9 and the previously described murine variant 7m8 (7) were packaged with a gamma-synuclein gene (SNCG) promoter to drive tdTomato expression in RGCs (19) and the pR1.

AAV2-LALIQDSMRA (designated NHP#9), the second ranking variant from the GFP-barcoded screen in RPE, was packaged at high titers ( $\sim5 \times 10^{13}$ vg/mL) and therefore selected for a first round of validation studies focusing on ganglion cells of the inner retina and cones of the outer retina.
Quantification of vector performance in the ONL revealed that AAV2-7-mer and LoopSwap-based variants outperformed other viruses (Table 1).

However, relative to this input, analysis of results from deep sequencing over the rounds of selection revealed a subset of variants that increased significantly in their representation during rounds of selection for each of the input libraries (Figure 3C).

Libraries were injected, harvested, and repackaged for up to 5 sequential rounds of selection, with 1 round of error-prone PCR performed after round 3 (Figure 2 and Supplemental Table 1; supplemental material available online with this article; https://doi.

Inherited retinal degenerations (RDs) are caused by mutations in more than 200 genes (1), the majority of which are expressed in photoreceptors or retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) of the outer retina, leading to vision loss and decreased quality of life (2, 3).
A summary of minor adverse events related to the procedures is summarized in Supplemental Table 1.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Efficient AAV-mediated gene delivery to the outer retina remains a significant obstacle for retinal gene therapies, especially via intravitreal injection in primates.

canonical_events.summary: Inefficient AAV gene delivery to outer retina via intravitreal injection in primates due to anatomical barriers.
Designed replication-incompetent AAV libraries with rep in trans strategy to enable safe in vivo directed evolution in primates.
Constructed and packaged replication-incompetent AAV libraries (AAV2-7-mer, Ancestral-7-mer, LoopSwap) for primate screen.
Performed 5 rounds of in vivo selection in primates: intravitreal injection, tissue harvesting, PCR recovery, recloning, repackaging, with error-prone PCR after round 3.
Deep sequencing of selection rounds revealed convergence and identified variants with significant fold increase, despite initial overrepresentation of some variants.
Determined that fold increase, not final frequency, is the optimal metric for identifying top-performing variants.
Secondary GFP-barcoded library screen of 12 top variants plus controls injected in primates to rank transduction efficiency in ONL and RPE.
Validated top variant NHP#9 (AAV2-LALIQDSMRA) in primate retina: showed 2-fold increase in foveal cones infected and ~12-fold increase in GFP expression vs 7m8, with reduced RGC transduction.
Validated top LoopSwap variant NHP#26 (AAV2 583 LQRGVRIPSVLEVNGQ) in primate retina: showed GFP expression in fovea, photoreceptors, Müller cells, and inner nuclear layer despite low titer.
Some top variants had low production titers (e.g., NHP#26 ~5e11 vg/mL), limiting validation dose.
Secondary screen performed on only n=1 primate; small number of variants validated; long-term expression and safety not assessed.
Immune suppression used in validation may affect translatability; optimal regimen not determined.

edges.reason: Problem of inefficient delivery motivates design of replication-incompetent libraries for safe evolution.
Design of replication-incompetent strategy enables construction of specific libraries.
Constructed libraries are used in the in vivo selection rounds.
Selection rounds generate samples for deep sequencing analysis.
Analysis of sequencing data leads to the insight that fold increase is the optimal metric.
Fold increase metric guides selection of variants for secondary barcoded screen.
Secondary screen identifies NHP#9 as a top variant for validation.
Secondary screen identifies NHP#26 as a top variant for validation.
NHP#26 showed good transduction but had low production titer, limiting its utility.
Validation studies reveal limitations in sample size and scope.
Validation required immune suppression, raising translatability concerns.

must_cover_points: Problem of inefficient AAV delivery to outer retina via intravitreal injection in primates.
Design of replication-incompetent libraries for safe in vivo evolution.
Construction of AAV2-7-mer, Ancestral-7-mer, and LoopSwap libraries.
Five rounds of in vivo selection with error-prone PCR after round 3.
Deep sequencing analysis revealing convergence and fold increase metric.
Secondary GFP-barcoded screen of 12 variants to rank transduction.
Validation of NHP#9 showing improved cone transduction and reduced RGC transduction.
Validation of NHP#26 showing broad retinal transduction despite low titer.
Limitations: low titer of some variants, n=1 for secondary screen, immune suppression needed.
